headline: Popular Weight-Loss Drug Shows Signs of Slowing Aging
meta_description: New research reveals semaglutide may slow biological aging markers by reducing inflammation and excess fat, offering hope beyond weight loss.
summary_short: A clinical trial found that semaglutide, the active ingredient in Ozempic and Wegovy, slowed biological aging markers in adults with HIV.
body:
Researchers at UC San Diego have uncovered an unexpected benefit of a widely prescribed weight-loss medication: it may help slow biological aging itself.

In a study published in Nature Communications, scientists examined 108 adults with HIV-associated lipohypertrophy, a condition causing excess abdominal fat. Half received weekly semaglutide injections, the active ingredient in popular drugs Ozempic and Wegovy, while the rest received placebo.

The results surprised even the researchers. Those treated with semaglutide showed a 9% slower pace of biological aging based on one measurement system, with effects visible across multiple aging markers tied to inflammation and organ health—including the brain, heart, and kidneys.

How does a weight-loss drug affect aging? The answer lies in what semaglutide removes: visceral fat deep around organs and chronic inflammation. Both accelerate aging, particularly in people with HIV who experience accelerated biological aging despite effective treatment.

"We are not saying that semaglutide reverses aging," cautioned first author Michael Corley from the Stein Institute for Research on Aging. "What we are seeing is a signal that it may slow some biological processes associated with aging."

The findings could eventually help the broader population, researchers say. Larger studies are now underway.
